schemaMeta.name: ecommerce
schemaMeta.description: Core e-commerce data warehouse schema powering analytics, reporting, and operational dashboards.

tables.name: orders
tables.description: Core transaction table — every purchase flows through here. Contains order status, timestamps, and customer references.

tables.name: customers
tables.description: Customer master data including profile, signup date, and lifetime metrics.

tables.name: order_items
tables.description: Line items for each order — links orders to products with quantity and pricing.

tables.name: payments
tables.description: Payment transactions linked to orders. Supports multiple payment methods per order.

tables.name: revenue_daily
tables.description: Daily aggregated revenue metrics by product category and customer segment.

script.speaker: Alex
script.text: Welcome to DataBard! Today we're looking at the ecommerce schema — 6 tables, health score 52 out of 100. Let's dig in.

script.speaker: Morgan
script.text: This one needs work. 3 failing tests and only 67% test coverage. Plus payments hasn't been updated in over 30 hours. Let's talk about what's broken.

script.speaker: Morgan
script.text: Let's start with the fires. There are 2 tables I'm worried about.

script.speaker: Morgan
script.text: orders is critical — the accepted_values test on status is failing, and 3 tables depend on it. order_items, payments, and revenue_daily all cascade from here.

script.speaker: Alex
script.text: For context, orders is the core transaction table. 2.4 million rows, every purchase flows through here. Sarah Chen owns it.

script.speaker: Morgan
script.text: order_items has a failing positive_quantity test. Someone's inserting zero or negative quantities. That's 8.9 million rows of line item data with bad records.

script.speaker: Morgan
script.text: payments has a failing payment_amount_positive test, and it hasn't been refreshed in over 30 hours. Mike Kumar owns this one — might want to check the pipeline.

script.speaker: Morgan
script.text: products has zero quality tests. No not_null, no unique checks, nothing. That's a table with no safety net.

script.speaker: Alex
script.text: Documentation is at 83% which isn't bad — only products is missing a description. But order_items and products have no owner assigned.

script.speaker: Alex
script.text: On the bright side — customers is solid. All 3 tests passing, well-documented, PII columns properly tagged. That's the standard.

script.speaker: Morgan
script.text: Speaking of PII — customers has email and full_name flagged as personal data. Make sure access policies and retention rules cover those columns.

script.speaker: Alex
script.text: Data flow: 6 lineage connections. orders is the busiest node with 4 connections — it feeds order_items, payments, and revenue_daily.

script.speaker: Morgan
script.text: All lineage is internal to this schema. That's good for isolation, but the orders table is a single point of failure for the entire downstream chain.

script.speaker: Alex
script.text: That's the ecommerce schema — health score 52, 6 tables, 11 tests. Thanks for listening.

script.speaker: Morgan
script.text: Priority one: fix those 3 failing tests on orders, order_items, and payments. Priority two: add tests to products. Priority three: check why payments is stale. Until next time.
